1: A type: “Hear this word — the funeral song I take up over you, O house of Israel.” Amos sings a qînāh (lament-dirge) over Israel while they are still alive — a prophetic speech-act that treats the coming disaster as already accomplished. The only comparable NT scene is Jesus weeping over Jerusalem: “Would that you, even you, had known on this day the things that make for peace! But now they are hidden from your eyes... because you did not know the time of your visitation” (Luke 19:41-44). Both Amos and Jesus lament the city that is heading toward avoidable destruction; both laments are simultaneously prophecies of what will come.
2: A type: “She has fallen, never to rise again — the virgin Israel; abandoned on her own land, with no one to lift her up.” The fallen-and-unlifted language is the starkest image of Israel’s coming defeat. Simeon’s prophecy applies the same falling/rising pattern to Christ’s effect: “this child is destined for the falling and rising of many in Israel” (Luke 2:34). The one who is destined to cause rising is also himself the risen one — Christ, who was himself brought low (“he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death,” Philippians 2:8) and raised by the Father (Philippians 2:9), becomes the one who lifts up the fallen.
3: A shadow: the city that sent out a thousand men will have a hundred survive; the city that sent out a hundred will have ten left. The decimation pattern is the covenant curse for military defeat (Leviticus 26:8 inverted). The remnant-motif again: ten percent survive. Paul quotes the remnant principle from Isaiah but grounds it in Christ: “Though the number of the sons of Israel be as the sand of the sea, only a remnant of them will be saved” (Romans 9:27, citing Isaiah 10:22). The reduction from thousands to hundreds to tens is the eschatological narrowing that makes Christ’s preservation of his flock all the more striking: “I shall lose nothing of all that he has given me” (John 6:39).
4: A fulfillment: “For this is what the LORD says to the house of Israel: Seek me and live.” The invitation is direct and unconditional — seek YHWH himself, not sanctuaries or ritual, and live. Christ issues the same invitation in its fullest form: “Truly, truly, I say to you, whoever hears my word and believes him who sent me has eternal life. He does not come into judgment, but has passed from death to life” (John 5:24). The “live” of Amos 5:4 is the ḥāyāh (life in its fullest covenant sense); the eternal life of John 5:24 is the eschatological fulfillment of exactly this promise.
5: A type: “Do not seek Bethel; do not go to Gilgal or travel to Beersheba — for Gilgal will certainly go into exile and Bethel will become a disaster.” The command to avoid the sanctuaries — which would seem to be the places to seek God — is the prophetic subversion of institutional religion when it has become a substitute for genuine encounter. Jesus extends this logic to all geographical-cultic centers: “Woman, believe me, the hour is coming when neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem will you worship the Father” (John 4:21). The sanctuary-without-God is replaced by worship in spirit and truth through Christ, who is himself the new temple (John 2:21).
6: A fulfillment: “Seek the LORD and live, or he will sweep through the house of Joseph like a fire, devouring it with no one to quench it for Bethel.” The double summons — seek and live — is the prophetic gospel before the gospel. The sweeping fire is the judgment that awaits those who refuse. The NT holds both: “For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life” (John 3:16) — the same life-or-perish structure, with Christ as the one who makes life possible and who will execute judgment on the unresponsive (John 3:36).
7: A type: “You who twist justice into bitter poison and hurl righteousness to the ground.” The twisting of justice (laʿanāh, wormwood — the bitter plant of curse) and the grounding of righteousness is the corruption of the covenant order. Christ comes to right exactly this inversion: “He will not break a bruised reed or quench a smoldering wick, until he brings justice to victory” (Matthew 12:20, citing Isaiah 42:3). The establishment of justice is the central task of the Servant; Amos’s charge against Israel makes Christ’s justice-work the necessary response to the world’s distortion of it.
8: A fulfillment: “He who made the Pleiades and Orion, who turns deep darkness into dawn and darkens the day into night, who calls for the waters of the sea and pours them out over the face of the earth — the LORD is his name.” This creator-doxology is applied in the NT to Christ without ambiguity: “For by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible” (Colossians 1:16). The one who made the star-clusters (Pleiades/Orion) and controls the hydrological cycle is the Lord who becomes flesh. The turning of darkness into dawn is the literal pattern of Easter morning — “early on the first day of the week, while it was still dark” (John 20:1), darkness turning into the dawn of resurrection.
9: A shadow: “He sends destruction flashing against the powerful so that ruin comes upon the fortified city.” The divine warrior motif — God who strikes the powerful and the fortified — is the inverse of human military power. The NT’s “foolishness of God” passage applies this: “God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong; God chose what is low and despised in the world” (1 Corinthians 1:27-28). Christ on the cross is the destruction that “flashes against the powerful” — the Roman imperial execution system is the fortified city that ruin comes upon, because what it intends as defeat becomes the world’s redemption.
10: A type: “They hate the judge who rebukes them at the gate and despise the one who tells the truth.” The gate was the ancient courtroom — rebuke at the gate is official judicial correction. The hating of the truth-teller is the pattern that Jesus embodies and predicts: “If I tell the truth, why do you not believe me? Whoever is of God hears the words of God. The reason why you do not hear them is that you are not of God” (John 8:46-47). The truth-speaker who is hated in Amos’s gate is the type of the Truth (John 14:6) who is hated by the world (John 15:18).
11: A type: “Because you crush the poor and impose a grain tax on him, you have built houses of dressed stone, but you will not live in them; you have planted delightful vineyards, but you will not drink their wine.” The covenant curse — you build but will not inhabit, you plant but will not eat — reverses the blessing of Deuteronomy 28:30-33 (which was a blessing for obedience). Christ pronounces the same reversal on those who build systems of exploitation: “Woe to you who are rich, for you have received your consolation” (Luke 6:24). The stone houses are abandoned; what endures is the house not built by human hands (2 Corinthians 5:1).
12: A type: “I know how numerous your crimes are and how great your sins — you who oppress the innocent, accept bribes, and deny justice to the needy at the gate.” The divine omniscience of Israel’s crimes — “I know” — establishes that the Judge is fully informed. The NT extends this: “Nothing in all creation is hidden from God’s sight. Everything is uncovered and laid bare before the eyes of him to whom we must give account” (Hebrews 4:13). The one who knows Israel’s crimes is Christ, who also knows his own people perfectly: “I know my own and my own know me” (John 10:14). The same divine knowledge that condemns the unjust assures the just.
13: A shadow: “Therefore the wise man stays silent at a time like this, for it is an evil time.” The silence of the wise — in an evil time when speech is dangerous — is a transitional verse. Jesus himself is silent before his accusers at key moments: “But he gave him no answer, not even to a single charge, so that the governor was greatly amazed” (Matthew 27:14). The silence of the servant under unjust power (Isaiah 53:7: “he opened not his mouth”) is the redemptive counterpart to Amos’s prudential silence — Christ’s silence is not self-protective but sacrificial.
14: A fulfillment: “Seek good and not evil, that you may live; and then the LORD, the God of armies, will be with you as you have said.” The conditional “God with us” — ʿimmanū ʾĕlōhîm in structure — anticipates Emmanuel. Isaiah gives it a name (Isaiah 7:14: ʿImmānūʾēl); Matthew identifies its fulfillment: “Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and they shall call his name Immanuel (which means, God with us)” (Matthew 1:23). Amos’s conditional “God will be with you if you seek good” becomes, in Christ, the unconditional gift: God with us in the incarnate Son, regardless of whether Israel has first earned the condition.
15: A fulfillment: “Hate evil and love good; establish justice at the gate — perhaps the LORD, the God of armies, will be gracious to the remnant of Joseph.” The “perhaps” (ʾûlay) of Amos — YHWH’s grace is uncertain given Israel’s track record — becomes certainty in Christ. “The remnant of Joseph” is the northern tribe stripped almost bare; Paul identifies the remnant chosen by grace: “So too at the present time there is a remnant, chosen by grace. But if it is by grace, it is no longer on the basis of works” (Romans 11:5-6). The “perhaps” of Amos is resolved into the certainty of election in Christ — grace is the ground, not the reward for hating evil.
16: A shadow: “Therefore in all the public squares there will be wailing, and in every street they will say, ‘Alas! Alas!’” The universal lament — wailing in every plaza and street — is the sound of the Day of the LORD falling on Israel. The NT reserves the same sound for the eschatological day: “Then all the tribes of the earth will mourn, and they will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven with power and great glory” (Matthew 24:30; citing Zechariah 12:10-14). The wailing is both Israel’s consequence for rejecting the prophetic word and the mourning of those who see Christ’s return as judgment rather than salvation.
17: A shadow: “There will be wailing in every vineyard, for I will pass through your midst, declares the LORD.” The passing-through of YHWH in judgment echoes the Passover — YHWH passed through Egypt to strike the firstborn (Exodus 12:12). Now YHWH passes through Israel in the same judgment-mode. The Passover-and-exodus logic is reversed: Israel is in the place of Egypt. Christ’s blood as Passover Lamb (1 Corinthians 5:7) is the means by which the destroyer passes over those who trust him — the one mark that makes the difference between the judgment that wails in the vineyard and the life that survives it.
18: A fulfillment: “Woe to you who long for the day of the LORD! Why would you want the day of the LORD? It is darkness, not light.” Israel expects the Day of the LORD to bring vindication against its enemies; Amos announces it brings judgment on Israel itself. The NT makes the same reversal about those who assume Christ’s return will vindicate their position: “Not everyone who says to me, ‘Lord, Lord,’ will enter the kingdom of heaven” (Matthew 7:21). The Day turns out to be darkness for those who expected light — a warning that applies to every generation that assumes proximity to the form of religion equals protection from its substance.
19: A shadow: “It will be like a man who fled from a lion and ran into a bear, or escaped into his house and put his hand on the wall and a serpent bit him.” The inescapability of the Day of the LORD — every escape route leads to a worse danger — is a darkly comic parable. The NT counterpart is Revelation’s fifth trumpet: “In those days people will seek death and will not find it. They will long to die, and death will flee from them” (Revelation 9:6). The extreme of the inescapable judgment that Amos describes in earthly terms becomes in Revelation the anguish of the final judgment. Christ alone is the door that opens to escape: “I am the door. If anyone enters by me, he will be saved” (John 10:9).
20: A shadow: “Is not the day of the LORD darkness and not light — pitch dark, with no gleam of brightness?” The definitive answer to the rhetorical question: absolute darkness, no exception. The cross was accompanied by exactly this literal darkness: “Now from the sixth hour there was darkness over all the land until the ninth hour” (Matthew 27:45). The cosmic darkness at the crucifixion is the Day of the LORD’s darkness falling on the Son — he bears the pitch-dark judgment so that those who trust him live in the light: “I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness” (John 8:12).
21: A fulfillment: “I hate and despise your religious festivals; I take no pleasure in your solemn gatherings.” The divine rejection of festival-worship divorced from covenant ethics is the sharpest prophetic critique of institutional religion. Jesus applies this directly: “You have made void the word of God for the sake of your tradition” (Matthew 15:6). His cleansing of the temple at Passover (John 2:13-17) is the enacted form of Amos’s declaration — the festival has become a system that God despises. What God desires instead is visible in Christ, who embodies both perfect worship and perfect justice.
22: A shadow: “Even when you bring me burnt offerings and grain offerings, I will not accept them; when you offer your fellowship offerings of fattened cattle, I will not look on them.” The refusal of Israel’s sacrifices is the inverse of the perfect sacrifice Christ offers. Hebrews makes this contrast explicit: “For it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins” (Hebrews 10:4) — not because sacrifice is wrong in principle, but because animal sacrifice divorced from the heart and from justice is what Amos condemns, and what Christ replaces with the one acceptable offering: himself (Hebrews 10:10: “by that will we have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all”).
23: A shadow: “Away with the noise of your songs! I will not listen to the music of your harps.” The silencing of Israel’s worship-music is the rejection of music performed by unjust hands. The NT offers the corrective: true worship is “singing and making melody to the Lord with your heart” (Ephesians 5:19) — heart-worship flowing from lives of justice and love, not external performance. Revelation’s vision of true worship (Revelation 5:9: “they sang a new song”) contrasts with Amos’s silenced harps: the new song is sung by those washed in Christ’s blood, whose lives the silenced harps pointed toward.
24: A fulfillment: “But let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream.” The most quoted verse in Amos is the positive vision after the negative rejection: what God wants is ceaseless, life-giving justice. The eschatological river of justice finds its NT home in both the kingdom of God and in Christ himself: “Whoever believes in me, as the Scripture has said, ‘Out of his heart will flow rivers of living water’” (John 7:38). Christ is the source from which justice-as-water flows. Revelation 22:1-2 pictures the river of life flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb — the ever-flowing stream of Amos 5:24 in its final eschatological form.
25: A shadow: “Did you bring me sacrifices and offerings during the forty years in the wilderness, O house of Israel?” The rhetorical question challenges Israel’s assumption that the sacrificial system is ancient and foundational. Stephen cites this verse in Acts 7:42-43 in his speech before the Sanhedrin, applying it to Israel’s history of apostasy and leading to his indictment: “you always resist the Holy Spirit” (Acts 7:51). The wilderness period was characterized more by grumbling and idol-worship than by faithful sacrifice — the point Amos makes and Stephen repeats in the face of the council that is about to stone him.
26: A fulfillment: “You carried your shrine of Sikkuth your king and Kiyyun your star-god — the idols you made for yourselves.” Stephen cites this verse (Amos 5:26) in Acts 7:43 (using the LXX, which reads “Moloch” and “Rephan”) as the culmination of Israel’s idolatry in the wilderness. The idolatry of star-worship — carrying astral-deity shrines while nominally worshiping YHWH — is the pattern of syncretism that the NT confronts in the form of Gentile idolatry (Acts 17:22-23: the Athenian altar to an unknown god; Paul redirects this to Christ) and Jewish compromise. The only true star-god is Christ, “the bright morning star” (Revelation 22:16).
27: A fulfillment-anticipation: “Therefore I will send you into exile beyond Damascus, says the LORD, whose name is the God of armies.” The exile-beyond-Damascus is the Assyrian exile of 722 BCE — the fate of the northern kingdom that refused to return to YHWH through five cycles of discipline (Amos 4). Stephen uses this verse (Acts 7:43 cites the LXX version “beyond Babylon”) to argue that Israel’s exile was the consequence of persistent idolatry, and that the Jerusalem establishment of his day is repeating the same pattern in rejecting Christ. The exile announced in Amos becomes in the NT the paradigm for understanding what it means to reject God’s appointed messengers — culminating in the rejection of the Messenger himself.
